metrics.narrative: Thomas McNulty articulated clear operational pain around spend classification accuracy and category lock timing on the 5 May discovery call: 'We need to be able to differentiate gas vs electric construction spend at the requisition stage and lock the category before PO creation. Right now our nomenclature is not accurate enough.' He framed this as critical for PSEG's dual utility operation (electric + gas). However, no quantified business impact was provided—no error rate, no cost of misclassification, no FTE burden, no cycle-time delay. When Ed asked about the size of PSEG's spend under management and current Ariba performance gaps, Thomas deferred ('I'd have to pull those numbers') and the conversation stayed qualitative. The pain is confirmed and operationally specific, but the financial case to justify $110k platform investment is not built. Without CFO/CPO engagement to anchor ROI (savings, efficiency gain, risk reduction), this stays an interesting operational improvement rather than a funded priority. Ed noted on the 16 Jun internal call that Thomas 'didn't start with a specific need in mind—this is informational,' reinforcing that the pain surfaced during discovery but was not the entry point. Quantifying the classification accuracy gap and its cost is the missing move to convert interest into business case.

champion.narrative: Thomas McNulty (Procurement Director, electric/gas construction procurement) is the sole champion, engaged across all five touchpoints (5 May discovery call, 16 Jun scheduled follow-up no-show, and three subsequent rep-initiated outreach attempts). On 5 May he was engaged, asked detailed questions about Zycus iAnalyze classification, CLM vs iRequest comparison, and utility taxonomy depth, and committed to internal socialization ('I'll socialize this with a few people'). He has operational pain (nomenclature accuracy, category lock timing) and owns the problem domain, which positions him as a credible advocate. However, his champion strength is developing-to-weak and currently cooling. Evidence of advocacy is thin: he has not introduced a single additional stakeholder in 60 days, has not reported back on internal socialization (promised 5 May, no follow-up as of 5 Jul), no-showed the 16 Jun call without reschedule, and has not answered subsequent calls or emails from Ed or Vinay. Access to Economic Buyer/Decision Maker is unproven—he has not mentioned CFO, CPO, or procurement leadership involvement, and Daniel Cregg (CFO) and Rory Caherly (Director Strategic Sourcing) remain unengaged. Thomas's role (Procurement Director for a function, not CPO or VP Procurement) suggests limited commercial authority; he can evaluate and recommend but likely cannot approve $110k spend alone. Current engagement: passive/cooling. The 16 Jun no-show and 19-day silence (as of 5 Jul) are red flags. Ed noted on the 16 Jun internal call, 'They take the first call, they're all excited, and then you never see them again'—a pattern Thomas is now following. What developed: strong operational interest and specific use-case questions on 5 May. What is missing: multi-threading, EB/DM access, internal advocacy traction, and sustained engagement. The champion is at risk of fading. Re-engagement within 7 days is critical; if Thomas does not reply, escalate to Rory Caherly or Daniel Cregg via LinkedIn/email to bypass the cooling thread.

competition.narrative: PSEG is evaluating Zycus as a replacement for SAP Ariba (incumbent S2P platform) and navigating recent internal adoption of iRequest (CLM) and Supplyhive (supplier performance management), both described by Thomas as 'fairly new—our Long Island peers have been using them for several years, we just started' on the 5 May call. Primary competitor is SAP Ariba (status quo, entrenched). Thomas did not state an Ariba renewal date or explicit dissatisfaction, but his questions about Zycus iAnalyze classification accuracy and taxonomy depth imply Ariba is underperforming on those dimensions. No head-to-head Ariba vs Zycus comparison has occurred; this is early exploration. Second competitor is iRequest (CLM), a recent costly switch that creates political and change-fatigue risk. Thomas asked for a detailed 'pros/cons comparison of Zycus iContract vs iRequest if starting from scratch,' signaling he is evaluating whether to consolidate CLM under Zycus or keep iRequest and integrate. Ed offered integration ('we could integrate to iRequest') as a hedge, but did not position the unified platform ROI case. Risk: procurement leadership may resist replacing a newly adopted CLM, favoring Ariba-only swap or leaving iRequest in place. Third competitor is Supplyhive (supplier performance management), also new at PSEG; no direct Zycus SPM positioning occurred on the 5 May call. Fourth competitor is a third-party spend analysis vendor (not named), mentioned by Thomas when describing PSEG's current stack. Fifth and most dangerous competitor: do-nothing / status quo. Thomas framed the engagement as 'informational, I didn't start with a specific need in mind' (per Ed, 16 Jun)—meaning he is exploring, not actively solving a funded problem. With no compelling event, no Ariba renewal deadline, and no exec mandate, inertia is the highest risk. How we win: Position Zycus as the unified S2P+CLM+analytics platform that eliminates PSEG's current multi-vendor complexity (Ariba + iRequest + third-party analytics + Supplyhive), delivers utility-specific taxonomy and gas/electric classification accuracy Ariba cannot, and future-proofs with Merlin/ANA agentic AI that iRequest and Ariba lack. Anchor the business case to measurable procurement accuracy improvement (reduce misclassification rate, quantify audit/budget-tracking cost savings) and total cost of ownership vs maintaining four vendors. Execute via ANA pilot to prove AI differentiation, then escalate to CFO/CPO with ROI narrative tied to the relevant stakeholder modernization timeline (if one exists). Wedge: AI-native platform with utility-grade taxonomy vs. patchwork legacy stack. Activation: make Merlin/ANA the Phase 2 centerpiece and use the pilot to create an internal champion story.

identify_pain.narrative: Thomas McNulty (Procurement Director, electric/gas construction) articulated specific operational pain on the 5 May discovery call, directly tied to PSEG's dual-utility structure. Pain point: 'Our nomenclature is not accurate enough—we need to be able to differentiate gas vs electric construction spend at the requisition stage and lock the category before PO creation.' This is a procurement data accuracy and spend visibility issue: misclassified construction spend creates reporting errors, budget tracking failures, and potentially compliance/audit risk for a regulated utility managing separate electric and gas capital budgets. Thomas owns this pain (he is responsible for construction procurement accuracy), and it is confirmed, not inferred. He also expressed interest in comparing Zycus CLM to iRequest, which PSEG 'just started' using—implying dissatisfaction or uncertainty about iRequest's fit, though he did not explicitly state iRequest pain (this is inferred). The third pain signal is implied by his question about Zycus's nomenclature/classification library size for utilities—suggesting PSEG's current Ariba taxonomy is either too generic or poorly maintained for their use case. All three pain points converge on a single theme: PSEG's current S2P stack (Ariba + iRequest + third-party spend analytics, per 5 May call) is not delivering the data accuracy, category control, and utility-specific taxonomy depth Thomas needs. However, Thomas framed the call as 'informational' (per Ed's recap on 16 Jun), meaning he did not enter with active pain—Ed surfaced it during discovery. This lowers urgency: the pain is real but not yet escalated to a funded initiative or exec-sponsored project. Quantifying the cost of nomenclature inaccuracy (FTE effort to correct, audit findings, budget variance) would elevate this from operational annoyance to business case.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is early-stage and undocumented. The call sequence shows single-persona discovery (5 May Thomas only, 16 Jun Thomas no-show, 10 Jul rep-initiated next-step with no buyer reply)—no stakeholder expansion, no exec escalation, no procurement/legal/InfoSec joining, no multi-person demo. Thomas mentioned on 5 May that he would 'socialize the deck with a few people internally' and then decide on an ANA pilot, but he did not name stakeholders, provide a timeline, or report back on that socialization as of 5 Jul (60 days later). The NextStep field (10 Jul) states 'Thomas will speak internally since Ed has already sent across the deck to him and depending on that discussion, they would be open to get started with an ANA pilot project'—this is still the internal socialization step, not a confirmed next milestone. No approval chain has been described: Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c, Multiple_approvals__c are all null in Salesforce. The evidence suggests this is informational exploration by an operational buyer (Thomas, Procurement Director) who has not yet engaged procurement leadership (Rory Caherly, Director Strategic Sourcing), finance (Daniel Cregg, CFO), or IT (for SAP ERP integration scoping). The process Thomas is running appears to be: (1) personal discovery with Zycus, (2) informal internal socialization (status unknown), (3) potential ANA pilot if socialization yields interest. This is not a structured enterprise procurement process (RFI/RFP/shortlist/exec approval) and lacks the milestones, stakeholders, and timeline required for a $110k platform purchase. The blocking move is to surface the formal process: ask Thomas directly who approves, what the evaluation stages are, and when the next multi-person session can be scheduled.

decision_criteria.narrative: Three specific evaluation criteria emerged from the 5 May discovery call, all operationally focused and tied to PSEG's SAP Ariba replacement evaluation: (1) Spend classification accuracy and category lock timing—Thomas needs real-time iAnalyze classification that can differentiate gas vs electric construction spend at requisition stage and lock category before PO creation, solving nomenclature accuracy gaps in current Ariba setup. (2) CLM capability vs iRequest—Thomas is comparing Zycus iContract to iRequest (the CLM PSEG's Long Island peers recently adopted and PSEG 'just started' using), asking for pros/cons comparison and data flow from eSourcing RFP to contract, plus clause-level risk scoring. He is evaluating whether to replace iRequest with the relevant stakeholder or integrate Zycus S2P to iRequest (Ed offered integration as an option). (3) Nomenclature/classification library depth for utilities—Thomas asked 'how large is the Zycus library for a utility of our size (electric + gas)?' indicating incumbent taxonomy breadth is a decision factor. These are confirmed, specific, and operationally grounded. However, no formal RFP/RFI process has started, no scoring rubric shared, no exec-level strategic criteria (ERP integration roadmap, AI/automation mandate, total cost of ownership) surfaced, and no multi-stakeholder use-case validation scheduled. The criteria are early-stage, single-persona (Thomas's operational view), and not yet weighted or formalized into a procurement decision framework. Moving to Shortlisted requires structured RFP positioning and expanding criteria capture to include CFO/CPO strategic priorities (ROI, risk, modernization alignment with the relevant stakeholder plans).

items.detail: Zero buyer-side contact roles on opp. Thomas McNulty (Procurement Director, electric/gas construction) is sole champion across all touchpoints. No stakeholders joined 5 May discovery call, no multi-person follow-up scheduled, and Thomas's internal socialization ('I'll socialize with a few people', 5 May) has produced no visible traction in 60 days. If Thomas fades, deal dies (single point of failure).

Economic Buyer not identified. Thomas is operational buyer (Procurement Director) but lacks commercial authority for $110k multi-year S2P platform. No CFO, CPO, or VP Procurement surfaced. Daniel Cregg (EVP & CFO) and Rory Caherly (Director Strategic Sourcing) on account contact list but not engaged. No exec sponsor for SAP ERP modernization (timing lever) identified. Without EB/exec access, deal stays informational and will not convert to active buying.

No compelling event or forcing function. Thomas: 'This is informational, I didn't start with a specific need in mind' (5 May). Ariba renewal date unknown (Thomas didn't know, Ed noted typical 12-18 month eval window but no date anchored). SAP ERP modernization mentioned but no timeline, owner, or mandate. Close date 1 May 2027 is credible for 12-15 month cycle IF momentum exists, but with no clock and passive interest do-nothing is highest threat.

iRequest (CLM) and Supplyhive (SPM) are 'fairly new' at PSEG ('our Long Island peers have been using them for several years, we just started', Thomas 5 May). Recent costly switch creates change fatigue and political resistance to another CLM replacement. Procurement leadership may favor Ariba-only swap or phased approach (defer CLM/SPM consolidation). Needs exec-level unified platform ROI case to overcome inertia.

Champion cooling after 16 Jun no-show. Thomas engaged strongly 5 May but no-showed 16 Jun follow-up, did not answer calls from Ed or Vinay, and has not re-engaged 19 days later (as of 5 Jul). Ed noted 'they take the first call, they're all excited, and then you never see them again' (16 Jun call). Risk: Thomas deprioritized internally (deck socialization got no traction, or competing priority took over) or lost interest (informational framing never converted to need). Without re-engagement in next 7 days, champion will fade and deal will go dark.

items.action: Address Thomas's three explicit requirements in a single consolidated document (1-page brief + 2-3 page appendix): (1) Zycus iAnalyze real-time classification for gas/electric construction spend differentiation and category lock at requisition stage (include utility peer example, screenshot of category lock UI, and nomenclature library size for PSEG scale). (2) Zycus iContract vs iRequest pros/cons comparison, eSourcing-to-contract data flow diagram, clause-level risk scoring demo. (3) Nomenclature/classification library depth for electric+gas utilities (quantify taxonomy coverage, compare to generic Ariba library). Deliver via email with subject 'PSEG S2P Eval: Your 3 Questions Answered' and request 15-minute call to walk through. Use as re-engagement content if rank-1 soft touch does not yield reply.

If combined with rank-1 or rank-2 outreach, increases reply likelihood. If Thomas does not engage, document becomes leave-behind for Caherly bypass (rank-2).

items.action: Create urgency by tying Zycus eval to external forcing function: research PSEG's SAP ERP modernization project timeline (check PSEG earnings calls, utility industry news, LinkedIn posts from PSEG IT/Procurement leaders) and Ariba contract expiration window. If SAP ERP timeline is public, frame next outreach (rank-1, rank-2, or rank-4) as 'SAP ERP modernization is the right moment to evaluate S2P platform alternatives—replacing Ariba now avoids costly re-integration later.' If no public timeline, ask directly on next call: 'Thomas mentioned SAP ERP modernization—what's the timeline, and who's leading that effort? We should align Zycus S2P eval with that project to streamline integration and avoid dual vendor transitions.' Goal: anchor eval to a real clock (ERP project or Ariba renewal) to counter do-nothing inertia.
items.trigger: No compelling event identified. Thomas: 'This is informational, I didn't start with a specific need in mind.' Ariba renewal date unknown, SAP ERP modernization mentioned but no timeline. Do-nothing is highest competitive threat; without external clock, eval will drift indefinitely.
items.expected_effect: If ERP timeline exists and is public/confirmed, positions Zycus eval as strategically timed (not optional nice-to-have) and creates urgency to move forward before ERP cutover. If no ERP timeline surfaces, shifts conversation to Ariba renewal: 'When does your Ariba contract expire? Let's make sure you have time to evaluate alternatives before auto-renewal.' Either path converts informational eval into time-bound decision. Raises win probability by 15-20% if forcing function is confirmed and internalized by buyer.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 8 May 2026, close date 1 May 2027 = 358-day cycle, credible for 12-15 month enterprise S2P platform sale. 58 days elapsed, 300 remaining. Stage: Qualified (discovery only). Time-in-stage: 58 days in Qualified is normal for early enterprise eval window, no concern alone. Pace required: to reach Shortlisted by Oct 2026 (5 months, typical for RFI/RFP/multi-stakeholder demos) deal needs RFI/RFP positioning, multi-person demos, and exec sponsorship by Sept. At current single-thread pace (one contact, informational frame, no next milestone scheduled), required velocity is not achievable. No forward slip (close date unchanged since creation), but absence of slip reflects lack of active milestones rather than momentum. Verdict driver: last two-way buyer engagement 5 May (Thomas discovery call), last activity 10 Jul (rep-initiated next-step update, no buyer reply), 60 days since first touch with zero stakeholder expansion or exec access = one active contributor (low engagement, single-threaded, cooling cadence). Forecast: Pipeline is appropriate; deal is not defensible for Best Case (no EB, no champion strength, no validated need). At Risk (not Off Track) because last touch is within 60 days and Thomas has not explicitly disengaged, but cooling signal is clear.

competitive_position.summary: Competing against SAP Ariba (entrenched incumbent S2P, underperforming on taxonomy/classification per Thomas's questions), iRequest (recently adopted CLM, change-fatigue risk), Supplyhive (new SPM), third-party spend analytics vendor, and do-nothing inertia (highest threat—no compelling event, informational eval). Zycus positioned as unified platform replacement (S2P+CLM+analytics) with utility-specific taxonomy, gas/electric classification accuracy, and Merlin/ANA AI differentiation. No head-to-head comparison run yet; Thomas is exploring, not active buying. Integration to iRequest offered as hedge but unified platform ROI case not built. Do-nothing wins if EB/exec sponsorship and business case do not materialize.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: PSEG's stated operational needs align well with Zycus iAnalyze (real-time spend classification, utility-specific taxonomy, gas/electric differentiation) and Merlin/ANA (AI-native capabilities iRequest and Ariba lack). Thomas's three explicit asks (classification accuracy, CLM comparison, taxonomy depth) are all addressable. Risk: expectations are single-persona (Thomas, operational buyer) and not validated with procurement leadership, CFO, or IT (SAP ERP integration stakeholders). If EB/DM priorities differ (e.g. lowest TCO vs best-of-breed, Ariba-only replacement vs full platform consolidation, defer CLM change to avoid iRequest disruption), current positioning may misalign. Need multi-stakeholder discovery to confirm fit at decision-maker level.

hard.ais_why:
* The proposed next step is an AI pilot: notes record "scheduling a follow-up... to discuss a four-week pilot for Autonomous Negotiation Agent" (Anna) to prove tail-spend savings - a concrete AI-forward motion.
* The session centered on Zycus AI: Merlin Intake conversational requisitions via Teams, iAnalyze real-time spend classification, a touchless invoicing agent, and a supplier-risk dashboard scraping thousands of sources.
* But customer-side enthusiasm is not yet strongly voiced and nothing is committed: the deck is being socialized with PSEG stakeholders with a follow-up in 1-2 weeks; no agreed pilot, POC, or budget yet. Qualified at 10%.

Also noted iRequest is 'fairly new—our Long Island peers have been using them for several years, we just started.'
value.source: Avoma 5 May 2026 discovery call
value.how_we_win: Careful positioning required. Recent costly switch to iRequest creates change fatigue and political resistance to another CLM replacement. Two paths: (1) Unified platform ROI—position Zycus iContract as integrated S2P+CLM with better eSourcing-to-contract data flow, clause-level risk scoring, and Merlin AI contract intelligence iRequest lacks, emphasizing TCO savings vs maintaining separate vendors. (2) Integration compromise—if procurement leadership resists ripping out new CLM, offer Zycus S2P integration to iRequest (as Ed suggested) and defer CLM consolidation to Phase 2. Path 1 is stronger long-term but requires exec-level business case to overcome inertia; test receptivity with Thomas and CFO before hard-positioning against iRequest.

value.name: Third-party spend analysis vendor
value.quote: We use a third party to do our spend analysis. Our nomenclature is very hard. We found it very difficult to get the nomenclature correct. I can buy something, a requisitioner can buy the same thing 10 times and call it 10 different things so when you're trying to say how much is my spend in overhead construction, how do I know I'm getting all of it? We give them April spend, they analyze it, massage it, come back probably in May with April spend classified so we can do spend analysis. The data is always a month behind. (Thomas McNulty, 5 May 2026).
value.source: Avoma discovery call 5 May 2026, Thomas McNulty
value.how_we_win: Current third-party process is offline, 1-month lag, requires monthly data extract/load, and relies on post-hoc cleanup (biweekly Zycus review vs real-time classification). Thomas explicitly pained by nomenclature inconsistency (gas vs electric construction categorization at requisition stage). Zycus iAnalyze with Autoclass AI auto-classifies spend in real time at requisition stage (system asks requisitioner 'is this gas or electric' if ambiguous, locks category before PO created) and surfaces cost-saving opportunities automatically. Ed confirmed Zycus performs biweekly spend cleanup as part of license (no separate vendor, no data extract/load). This is a clear win vs current state; make real-time classification and biweekly Zycus-owned cleanup a demo/proof point in ANA pilot or follow-on use-case session.

value.name: Do-nothing / status quo
value.quote: Ed (16 Jun internal call, recounting Thomas): 'This is informational, I didn't start with a specific need in mind.' Also: 'They take the first call, they're all excited, and then you never see them again.' Thomas no-showed 16 Jun follow-up and has not re-engaged 19 days later.
value.source: Avoma 16 Jun 2026 internal call; Avoma call log and Next Step History through 5 Jul
value.how_we_win: Highest threat. No compelling event (Ariba renewal date unknown, SAP ERP modernization timeline unclear, no exec mandate), no EB/exec sponsorship, single cooling champion, informational frame not converted to active buying. Do-nothing wins by default if momentum does not materialize. Counter: (1) Re-engage Thomas within 7 days with value-add (e.g. utility peer reference, Merlin demo tailored to gas/electric classification). (2) If Thomas does not reply, bypass to Rory Caherly (Director Strategic Sourcing) or Daniel Cregg (CFO) via LinkedIn/email. (3) Create urgency: tie Zycus eval to SAP ERP modernization timeline (if one exists) or position ANA pilot as low-risk fast proof (4-6 week pilot, confined scope, measurable outcome). (4) Quantify cost of status quo (procurement accuracy errors, FTE burden, audit risk) to build business case. Without near-term re-engagement and EB access, do-nothing wins and deal goes dark.

value.name: Do-nothing / status quo
value.quote: This is informational at this point. I didn't start with a specific need in mind. Chris convinced me I should at least listen to your pitch. This is your opportunity to tell me what you do and what you might be able to do for PSEG. (Thomas McNulty, 5 May 2026). Follow-up 16 Jun: Thomas no-show, no reply to calls. Ed: 'They take the first call, they're all excited, and then you never see them again.' (16 Jun 2026).
value.source: Avoma discovery call 5 May 2026 and follow-up call 16 Jun 2026, Thomas McNulty no-show
value.how_we_win: Do-nothing is strongest current threat because no compelling event identified (Ariba renewal date unknown, SAP modernization timeline/owner undefined, no urgent business case), single-threaded to Thomas (zero exec sponsorship), and Thomas framed as passive discovery not active buying. 16 Jun no-show + no re-engagement = cooling risk. Counter with: (1) re-engage Thomas in next 7 days with value-add trigger (ANA pilot ROI one-pager, Ariba-replacement case study, Colombia risk scenario proof point, or SAP modernization tie-in research); (2) multi-thread to peer/manager (Rory Caherly Director Strategic Sourcing, Joseph Lamotta Sr. Manager Procurement COE, Ryan Kral Director Procurement); (3) anchor to forcing function (find Ariba renewal date or SAP modernization milestone, position ANA pilot as low-risk proof before renewal decision); (4) convert articulated pain into quantified business case (bolt-on cost + AP manual burden + tail-spend leakage + nomenclature/lag cost + risk blind spot impact = $X annually, Zycus saves $Y, payback Z months). Without these, inertia wins.
